chickens.text: Begging academics to include methodology with their twenty post "I tested an LLM and this is how it failed" threads, I just saw a huge thread on how they fail at summarizing literature and it turns out the author wasn't actually providing the text, just asking it for summaries based on training data
if the 5.6 benchmarks are to be believed, i do not see how anthropic maintains corporate buy in for much longer without significantly cutting token costs

scenes.alt: A meme of a person saying to a computer "improve yourself." The computer improves itself and the person says "oh my god."

followed by smirk: they are queer folk, these talkies, very queer. theirs is a curious patter, a kind of chaffering hubbub, if you like. they seem well enough, and yet they have a constraint, a hesitance, something unlike your everyday talk. i think, though, they'll brighten. they'll improve. they're friendly enough, willing to share your cup, if you'll let them. they make odd propositions, but I don't like to refuse them; they seem so lonely. I rather like them, after all. they amuse me, if they mystify, and that, from a homeless one, is something gained. I like their chat, and their broken serenade, and their queer imperfect syntax. I like to hear them, and to watch them walking. they cheer the Temporal. that's how I take them; and, for talkie,
that may do. they're queer, but they're friendly; so, what for more?

C/C++ with static code analysis is not worse than Rust. But most Rust developers are beginners during there are many C/C++ developers with 20+ years knowledge. So whats exactly is the point in using Rust?

Stick figure meme comic. A person says 'improve meme' to a computer, which 'improves meme.' The computer screen shows the same meme rendered inside it (recursively), with the person saying 'oh my god' in shock. The recursive image within the image is faint but shows the same speech bubble structure.

But supposing the world has become “filled up”, so to speak, with liberal democracies, such as there exist no tyranny and oppression worthy of the name against which to struggle? Experience suggests that if men cannot struggle on behalf of a just cause because that just cause was victorious in an earlier generation, then they will struggle against the just cause. They will struggle for the sake of struggle. They will struggle, in other words, out of a certain boredom: for they cannot imagine living in a world without struggle. And if the greater part of the world in which they live is characterized by peaceful and prosperous liberal democracy, then they will struggle against that peace and prosperity, and against democracy.

• Chat: Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise users access GPT-5.6
Sol through medium and higher effort settings. Pro and Enterprise users can also select GPT-5.6 Sol Pro for the highest-quality results on complex tasks.
• ChatGPT Work and Codex: Free and Go users access GPT-5.6 Terra.
Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise users can choose among GPT-5.6
Sol, Terra, and Luna and set an effort level for each. max is available to all users with access to GPT-5.6 in ChatGPT Work and Codex and can be toggled on in settings. In ChatGPT Work, ultra is available to Pro and Enterprise users. In Codex, it is available to Plus and higher plans.

A performance benchmark comparison table evaluating five AI models—Muse Spark 1.1 (Meta), Muse Spark (Meta), Gemini 3.1 Pro (high) (Google), Opus 4.8 (max) (Anthropic), and GPT 5.5 (xhigh) (OpenAI)—across Agent, Coding, and Multimodal categories. Muse Spark 1.1 performance is highlighted in a dedicated blue column.
Agent Benchmarks
 * MCP Atlas (Scaled tool use): Muse Spark 1.1 leads at 88.1, outperforming Opus 4.8 (82.2), Muse Spark (82.2), Gemini 3.1 Pro (78.2), and GPT 5.5 (75.3).
 * JobBench (Professional tool use): Muse Spark 1.1 leads decisively at 54.7, significantly outscoring Opus 4.8 (48.4), GPT 5.5 (38.3), Muse Spark (17.0), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (15.9).
 * Toolathlon-Verified (Personal tool use): Muse Spark 1.1 scores 75.6, narrowly trailing the top performer Opus 4.8 (76.2), while beating GPT 5.5 (73.5), Gemini 3.1 Pro (61.1), and Muse Spark (49.4).
 * OSWorld-Verified (Agentic computer use): Muse Spark 1.1 scores 80.8, trailing Opus 4.8 (83.4) but leading GPT 5.5 (78.7), Gemini 3.1 Pro (76.2), and Muse Spark (53.3).
 * Humanity's Last Exam (Multidisciplinary reasoning w/ tools): Muse Spark 1.1 leads at 62.1, ahead of Opus 4.8 (57.9), GPT 5.5 (52.2), Gemini 3.1 Pro (51.4), and Muse Spark (50.4).
 * Finance Agent v2 (Agentic financial analysis): Muse Spark 1.1 leads at 57.2, outperforming Opus 4.8 (53.9), GPT 5.5 (51.8), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (43.0). Muse Spark has no listed data.
Coding Benchmarks
 * Terminal-Bench 2.1 (Agentic terminal coding): Muse Spark 1.1 scores 80.0, placing behind GPT 5.5 (83.4) and Opus 4.8 (82.7), but beating Gemini 3.1 Pro (70.3) and Muse Spark (67.3).
 * SWE-Bench Pro (Diverse software engineering): Muse Spark 1.1 scores 61.5, trailing Opus 4.8 (69.2) but outscoring GPT 5.5 (58.6), Muse Spark (55.0), and Gemini 3.1 Pro (54.2).
 * DeepSWE 1.1 (Long-horizon agentic coding): Muse Spark 1.1 scores 53.3, behind GPT 5.5 (67.0) and Opus 4.8 (59.0), but demonstrating a massive jump over Gemini 3.1 Pro (12.0) and base Muse Spark (10.0).
